content:
# Joshua Chapter 5 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), folio pages 228–229 | Codex Vaticanus (LXX)
**Source Images:** `JOS_005_page_001.jpg`, `JOS_005_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 165–166 | **Source Image:** `JOS_005_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`

---

**Manuscript Confirmation:** The Leningrad Codex images (Pages 228–229, West Semitic Research Project, (c) Bruce E. Zuckerman) have been read directly by all three council translators. Page 228 (JOS_005_page_001.jpg) displays the characteristic three-column layout with full niqqud and te'amim. Joshua 5 begins following the conclusion of Joshua 4, opening with the report of the Amorite and Canaanite kings' terror (v. 1), continuing through the circumcision command and execution (vv. 2–3), the parenthetical explanation of the two generations (vv. 4–7), and the healing at Gilgal and the rolling away of reproach (vv. 8–9). Page 229 (JOS_005_page_002.jpg) carries the Passover observance (v. 10), the eating of the land's produce (v. 11), the cessation of the manna (v. 12), and the theophanic encounter with the commander of YHWH's army (vv. 13–15), after which Joshua 6 begins following a parasha break. The Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209, Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana), page 165 (JOS_005_vaticanus_page_001.jpg), carries material from the end of Joshua 4 into Joshua 5 in continuous uncial script without verse divisions; the LXX text is in general agreement with the MT throughout, with notable divergences catalogued below.

---

*The Kings Hear and Their Hearts Melt (v. 1)*

**5:1** And it was, when all the kings of the Amorites who were beyond the Jordan to the west, and all the kings of the Canaanites who were by the sea, heard that YHWH had dried up the waters of the Jordan before the sons of Israel until they had crossed over, that their hearts melted, and there was no longer spirit in them because of the sons of Israel.

---

*YHWH Commands the Second Circumcision (vv. 2–3)*

**5:2** At that time YHWH said to Joshua, "Make for yourself flint knives and circumcise the sons of Israel again, a second time."

**5:3** So Joshua made for himself flint knives and circumcised the sons of Israel at the Hill of the Foreskins.

---

*The Reason for the Second Circumcision (vv. 4–7)*

**5:4** And this is the reason that Joshua circumcised them: all the people who came out of Egypt — the males — all the men of war — had died in the wilderness on the way after they came out of Egypt. **5:5** For all the people who came out were circumcised, but all the people who were born in the wilderness on the way as they came out of Egypt had not been circumcised. **5:6** For forty years the sons of Israel walked in the wilderness, until all the nation — the men of war who came out of Egypt — were finished, those who did not listen to the voice of YHWH, to whom YHWH swore that he would not let them see the land that YHWH had sworn to their fathers to give to us — a land flowing with milk and honey. **5:7** And their sons he raised up in their place — them Joshua circumcised, for they were uncircumcised, because they had not been circumcised on the way.

---

*The Reproach of Egypt Rolled Away (vv. 8–9)*

**5:8** And it was, when all the nation had finished being circumcised, that they remained in their places in the camp until they were healed.

**5:9** And YHWH said to Joshua, "Today I have rolled away the reproach of Egypt from upon you." So the name of that place has been called Gilgal to this day.

---

*The Passover at Gilgal and the Manna Ceases (vv. 10–12)*

**5:10** And the sons of Israel encamped at Gilgal, and they kept the Passover on the fourteenth day of the month in the evening, in the plains of Jericho.

**5:11** And they ate from the produce of the land on the day after the Passover — unleavened cakes and parched grain — on this very day.

**5:12** And the manna ceased on the day after they ate from the produce of the land, and there was no longer manna for the sons of Israel. And they ate from the yield of the land of Canaan in that year.

---

*The Commander of the Army of YHWH (vv. 13–15)*

**5:13** And it was, when Joshua was by Jericho, that he lifted up his eyes and looked, and behold — a man standing before him with his drawn sword in his hand. And Joshua went to him and said to him, "Are you for us, or for our adversaries?"

**5:14** And he said, "No — for I am the commander of the army of YHWH. Now I have come." And Joshua fell on his face to the earth and bowed down, and said to him, "What does my lord speak to his servant?"

**5:15** And the commander of the army of YHWH said to Joshua, "Remove your sandal from upon your foot, for the place on which you are standing is holy." And Joshua did so.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### On the Architecture of Joshua 5 — Three Movements of Threshold

All three council translators identified the same tripartite structure in Joshua 5, and the council adopts their shared analysis. This chapter stands as one of the most transitional in the Hebrew Bible — a sequence of thresholds between the wilderness and the land:

**Movement I — Circumcision at Gilgal (vv. 1–9): Restoration of Covenant Identity.** At the moment of maximum enemy weakness (the kings' hearts have melted), YHWH commands not attack but circumcision — rendering the entire army vulnerable. Covenant faithfulness precedes military action. The generation that enters the land must bear the covenant sign in their flesh, as Abraham's household did when first commanded (Genesis 17). The parenthetical explanation in vv. 4–7 carefully distinguishes the exodus generation (circumcised, disobedient, dead) from the wilderness-born generation (uncircumcised, untested, alive). The circumcision at Gilgal reconstitutes Israel as a covenant people. YHWH then declares the "reproach of Egypt" rolled away — the galal/Gilgal wordplay forming an explicit etiology.

**Movement II — Passover and the Cessation of Manna (vv. 10–12): Transition of Provision.** The Passover at Gilgal is the third recorded Passover: Egypt (Exodus 12), Sinai (Numbers 9), Gilgal (Joshua 5). The progression — deliverance, law, inheritance — marks each Passover as a covenantal milestone. The cessation of the manna (vayyishbot, from the root shavat — the manna "sabbathed," it rested) is seamless: heaven's provision ceases precisely when the land's provision begins, with no gap between.

**Movement III — The Commander of YHWH's Army (vv. 13–15): Theophanic Commission.** The encounter with the sword-bearing figure parallels Moses at the burning bush (Exodus 3) in nearly every element: theophanic figure, identity revealed, command to remove sandals, declaration of holy ground, mission commission. Joshua is placed in Moses's role. The "No" that refuses Joshua's binary question reframes the entire conquest: Israel does not have a divine ally helping them fight their war — YHWH is conducting his own campaign, and Israel participates in it.

---

### Verse-by-Verse Council Decisions

**5:1 — "their hearts melted" vs. "their hearts melted. They had no fight left in them"**

All three translators render the heart-melting image from the verb masas. EMET and LOGOS produce substantially identical renderings. PNEUMA splits the verse into shorter sentences and renders "no longer spirit in them" as "They had no fight left in them." The council adopts the EMET/LOGOS phrasing ("there was no longer spirit in them") as closer to the Hebrew ruach, which carries the sense of inner breath and resolve — richer than "fight." PNEUMA's rendering is vivid but narrows the semantic range. All three correctly follow the Qere (avoram, "they crossed") over the Ketiv (overenu, "we crossed"). See Disagreement D-1.

**5:1 — "to the west" vs. "to the sea" vs. "west of the Jordan"**

EMET renders yamah literally as "to the sea." LOGOS renders "to the west" (the functional meaning, since yamah indicates westward direction). PNEUMA renders "west of the Jordan." The council adopts "to the west" — yamah here functions as a directional indicator ("seaward" = "westward"), and "to the west" is both accurate and clear. See Disagreement D-2.

**5:2 — "flint knives" vs. "knives of flint"**

EMET preserves the Hebrew construct order: "knives of flint" (charvot tsurim). LOGOS and PNEUMA reverse to natural English: "flint knives." The council adopts "flint knives" — the English attributive construction is the standard handling of Hebrew construct chains and does not lose meaning. All three correctly identify tsurim as flint specifically, not merely "stone" (as in the LXX's petrinos). See Disagreement D-3.

**5:2 — "again, a second time" vs. "again"**

EMET and LOGOS both preserve the double emphasis of shuv + shenit ("again, a second time"). PNEUMA renders only "again," omitting shenit. The council preserves both markers — "again, a second time" — because the Hebrew text deliberately doubles the emphasis, and both words are present in the Leningrad Codex. See Disagreement D-4.

**5:4 — "who came out" vs. "who went out"**

EMET renders ha-yotse as "who went out." LOGOS and PNEUMA render "who came out." Both are valid renderings of yatsa. The council adopts "who came out" as the more natural English phrasing for departure from Egypt viewed from the narrative's perspective in Canaan. See Disagreement D-5.

**5:6 — "to give to us" vs. "to give us"**

EMET renders latet-lanu as "to give to us." LOGOS renders "to give us." PNEUMA paraphrases more freely ("he had promised their ancestors"). The council adopts "to give to us" — preserving the striking first-person plural lanu, which is a notable narrative voice shift (the narrator identifies with the recipients of the promise). See Disagreement D-6.

**5:7 — Hebrew word order and emphasis**

EMET preserves the marked Hebrew word order: "them Joshua circumcised" (otam mal Yehoshua). LOGOS normalizes to "these Joshua circumcised." PNEUMA freely restructures: "whom Joshua circumcised." The council adopts a rendering that preserves the emphatic fronting while remaining readable: "them Joshua circumcised." The fronted pronoun is a deliberate emphasis in the Hebrew — these sons, not the fathers. See Disagreement D-7.

**5:8 — "they recovered" vs. "they were healed" vs. "they had healed"**

EMET renders ad chayotam as "until they recovered." LOGOS renders "until they were healed." PNEUMA renders "until they had healed." The Hebrew chayah ("to live, to be restored to health") carries the sense of being made whole again. The council adopts "until they were healed" — it captures the medical sense of recovery from circumcision while preserving the passive nuance. See Disagreement D-8.

**5:9 — "reproach" vs. "disgrace"**

EMET and LOGOS both render cherpat Mitsrayim as "the reproach of Egypt." PNEUMA renders "the disgrace of Egypt," arguing that "disgrace" carries more emotional weight. The council adopts "reproach" — cherpah is a standard biblical term rendered "reproach" across the Hebrew Bible, and the word carries its full weight in English: a shame cast upon someone by another. "Disgrace" tends more toward self-inflicted shame in English usage. "Reproach" preserves the external, accusatory quality of the Hebrew. See Disagreement D-9.

**5:9 — "So the name" vs. "And he called the name" vs. "And so that place was called"**

EMET renders with full Hebrew structure: "And he called the name of that place Gilgal." LOGOS renders "So the name of that place has been called Gilgal." PNEUMA renders "And so that place was called Gilgal." The council adopts LOGOS's phrasing — it conveys the etiological force of the naming and the persistence of the name ("has been called ... to this day") while remaining clear. See Disagreement D-10.

**5:10 — "kept the Passover" vs. "made the Passover" vs. "celebrated the Passover"**

EMET renders vayya'asu et-hapesach as "they made the Passover" (literal asah). LOGOS renders "they kept the Passover." PNEUMA renders "they celebrated the Passover." The council adopts "they kept the Passover" — "kept" is the traditional English rendering for observing a festival and matches the sense of asah in liturgical context. See Disagreement D-11.

**5:11 — "parched grain" vs. "roasted grain"**

EMET and LOGOS render qalui as "parched grain." PNEUMA renders "roasted grain." The Hebrew qalui means grain that has been dried/roasted by heat. "Parched" is more technically precise (dried grain roasted over flame), while "roasted" is more accessible. The council adopts "parched grain" — it is the standard biblical rendering and distinguishes qalui from ordinary roasting. See Disagreement D-12.

**5:12 — "the manna ceased" vs. "the manna stopped"**

EMET and LOGOS render vayyishbot as "the manna ceased." PNEUMA renders "the manna stopped," arguing that "ceased" is overly formal. The council adopts "ceased" — the verb shavat carries the resonance of sabbath-rest and completion, not merely stopping. "Ceased" in English preserves more of this gravitas than "stopped," which implies an abrupt halt. All three translators note the shavat/Shabbat root connection. See Disagreement D-13.

**5:13 — "behold" vs. "look" vs. (omitted)**

EMET renders v'hinneh as "and look." LOGOS renders "and behold." PNEUMA omits the presentative particle, integrating it into the narrative flow: "he looked up and saw a man standing in front of him." The council adopts "behold" — hinneh is a narrative presentative that signals surprise and immediacy. "Behold" preserves this function in a way that mere description does not. See Disagreement D-14.

**5:13 — "Are you for us, or for our adversaries?" vs. "Are you on our side, or on theirs?"**

EMET and LOGOS render halanu attah im-letsarenu as "Are you for us, or for our adversaries?" PNEUMA renders "Are you on our side, or on theirs?" The council adopts the EMET/LOGOS rendering — "for our adversaries" preserves the Hebrew tsarenu ("our adversaries, our foes"), which is a concrete noun, not the vague "theirs." The binary must be stated in full to feel the force of the "No" that refuses it. See Disagreement D-15.

**5:14 — "No" vs. "Neither"**

EMET and LOGOS render lo as "No." PNEUMA renders "Neither," arguing that English "No" sounds like a refusal to answer rather than a refusal of the binary. The council adopts "No" — the Hebrew is a single stark syllable, lo, and it must retain its abruptness. The explanatory ki-clause that follows ("for I am the commander of the army of YHWH") clarifies the sense. "Neither" is interpretive and softens the refusal. The Hebrew does not say "neither" (lo zeh v'lo zeh); it says "No." See Disagreement D-16.

**5:14 — "bowed down" vs. "in reverence"**

EMET and LOGOS render vayyishtachu as "bowed down." PNEUMA renders "in reverence." The council adopts "bowed down" — vayyishtachu is a physical action (prostration), and the Hebrew describes the bodily posture, not the inner disposition. "In reverence" interprets motive; "bowed down" describes action. See Disagreement D-17.

**5:14 — "What does my lord speak to his servant?" vs. "What does my lord wish to say to his servant?"**

EMET and LOGOS render mah adoni medabber el-avdo as "What does my lord speak to his servant?" PNEUMA renders "What does my lord wish to say to his servant?" The council adopts the EMET/LOGOS rendering — medabber is a participle meaning "speaking," not "wishing to say." The Hebrew is direct: what is my lord saying? PNEUMA adds a volitional nuance not present in the text. See Disagreement D-18.

**5:15 — "Remove your sandal from upon your foot" vs. "Take off your sandal"**

EMET and LOGOS render shal na'alkha me'al raglekha as "Remove your sandal from upon your foot." PNEUMA renders "Take off your sandal," omitting "from upon your foot." The council adopts the full rendering — the prepositional phrase me'al raglekha ("from upon your foot") is present in the Hebrew and is significant because it preserves the singular number (foot, not feet), which distinguishes this command from the plural in Exodus 3:5. Omitting it loses a textual datum. All three translators note the singular vs. plural distinction from Exodus 3:5. See Disagreement D-19.

**5:15 — "the place on which you are standing is holy" vs. "the place upon which you are standing — holy it is"**

EMET preserves the Hebrew predicate-fronted structure: "the place upon which you are standing — holy it is" (qodesh hu). LOGOS normalizes: "the place on which you are standing is holy." PNEUMA simplifies: "The place where you are standing is holy." The council adopts the LOGOS phrasing — it preserves the full clause while rendering natural English. The predicate-fronting of EMET is a Hebrew syntactic feature that, while valuable in a literal translation, is not necessary for the consensus text. See Disagreement D-20.

---

## Disagreement Log

### D-1 | Verse 5:1 | "spirit" vs. "fight"
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "there was no longer spirit in them" | וְלֹא-הָיָה בָם עוֹד רוּחַ |
| LOGOS | "there was no longer any spirit in them" | same |
| PNEUMA | "They had no fight left in them" | same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"there was no longer spirit in them"** | |
**Rationale:** Ruach encompasses breath, spirit, resolve, and will — not merely "fight." The broader term preserves the Hebrew's semantic range. PNEUMA's rendering is vivid but reductive.

### D-2 | Verse 5:1 | "to the west" vs. "to the sea"
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "to the sea" | יָמָּה |
| LOGOS | "to the west" | same |
| PNEUMA | "west of the Jordan" | same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"to the west"** | |
**Rationale:** Yamah functions here as a directional indicator. "To the sea" is etymologically accurate but potentially confusing (which sea?). "To the west" conveys the intended geographical meaning.

### D-3 | Verse 5:2 | "flint knives" vs. "knives of flint"
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "knives of flint" | חַרְבוֹת צֻרִים |
| LOGOS | "flint knives" | same |
| PNEUMA | "flint knives" | same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"flint knives"** | |
**Rationale:** Two-to-one agreement. The English attributive construction is standard for Hebrew construct chains and loses no meaning.

### D-4 | Verse 5:2 | "again, a second time" vs. "again"
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "return, circumcise ... a second time" | וְשׁוּב מֹל ... שֵׁנִית |
| LOGOS | "circumcise ... again, a second time" | same |
| PNEUMA | "circumcise ... again" | same (omits שֵׁנִית) |
| **SOPHIA** | **"circumcise ... again, a second time"** | |
**Rationale:** Both shuv and shenit are present in the Leningrad Codex. The double emphasis is deliberate. PNEUMA's omission loses a word that is in the text.

### D-5 | Verse 5:4 | "came out" vs. "went out"
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "who went out from Egypt" | הַיֹּצֵא מִמִּצְרַיִם |
| LOGOS | "who came out of Egypt" | same |
| PNEUMA | "who had come out of Egypt" | same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"who came out of Egypt"** | |
**Rationale:** Yatsa can be "go out" or "come out." From the narrative vantage point in Canaan, "came out" is more natural. Minor divergence.

### D-6 | Verse 5:6 | "to give to us" vs. "to give us"
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "to give to us" | לָתֶת-לָנוּ |
| LOGOS | "to give us" | same |
| PNEUMA | (paraphrased: "he had promised their ancestors") | same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"to give to us"** | |
**Rationale:** The first-person plural lanu is a striking narrative voice shift — the narrator identifying with the promise recipients. "To give to us" preserves this emphasis more explicitly than "to give us."

### D-7 | Verse 5:7 | Word order — "them Joshua circumcised"
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "them Joshua circumcised" | אֹתָם מָל יְהוֹשֻׁעַ |
| LOGOS | "these Joshua circumcised" | same |
| PNEUMA | "whom Joshua circumcised" | same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"them Joshua circumcised"** | |
**Rationale:** The Hebrew fronts the object pronoun for emphasis. EMET's rendering preserves this marked word order most faithfully. The emphasis distinguishes the sons from the fathers who died in the wilderness.

### D-8 | Verse 5:8 | "recovered" vs. "were healed" vs. "had healed"
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "until they recovered" | עַד חֲיוֹתָם |
| LOGOS | "until they were healed" | same |
| PNEUMA | "until they had healed" | same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"until they were healed"** | |
**Rationale:** Chayah ("to live, to be restored") here refers to recovery from circumcision. "Were healed" captures the medical sense. "Recovered" is also valid; "had healed" is less precise (chayah is not the same as rapha).

### D-9 | Verse 5:9 | "reproach" vs. "disgrace"
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the reproach of Egypt" | חֶרְפַּת מִצְרַיִם |
| LOGOS | "the reproach of Egypt" | same |
| PNEUMA | "the disgrace of Egypt" | same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"the reproach of Egypt"** | |
**Rationale:** Two-to-one agreement. Cherpah denotes a shame imposed from outside — a taunt, a scorn. "Reproach" preserves this external accusatory quality. "Disgrace" leans toward self-inflicted shame.

### D-10 | Verse 5:9 | Etiological formula rendering
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "And he called the name of that place Gilgal until this day" | וַיִּקְרָא שֵׁם הַמָּקוֹם הַהוּא גִּלְגָּל עַד הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה |
| LOGOS | "So the name of that place has been called Gilgal to this day" | same |
| PNEUMA | "And so that place was called Gilgal — and it is called that to this day" | same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"So the name of that place has been called Gilgal to this day"** | |
**Rationale:** LOGOS's rendering captures both the etiological naming and the persistence of the name in idiomatic English. EMET's "And he called" is more literal but leaves the subject ambiguous (who called it?). PNEUMA's split sentence adds words not in the Hebrew.

### D-11 | Verse 5:10 | "kept" vs. "made" vs. "celebrated"
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "they made the Passover" | וַיַּעֲשׂוּ אֶת-הַפֶּסַח |
| LOGOS | "they kept the Passover" | same |
| PNEUMA | "they celebrated the Passover" | same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"they kept the Passover"** | |
**Rationale:** Asah with Pesach is a fixed biblical idiom for Passover observance. "Kept" is the traditional English rendering for festival observance and fits the liturgical register. "Made" is too literal; "celebrated" adds festive connotation not necessarily in the text.

### D-12 | Verse 5:11 | "parched grain" vs. "roasted grain"
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "parched grain" | קָלוּי |
| LOGOS | "parched grain" | same |
| PNEUMA | "roasted grain" | same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"parched grain"** | |
**Rationale:** Two-to-one agreement. Qalui refers to grain dried and roasted over flame — "parched" is the standard biblical rendering and more precise than "roasted."

### D-13 | Verse 5:12 | "ceased" vs. "stopped"
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the manna ceased" | וַיִּשְׁבֹּת הַמָּן |
| LOGOS | "the manna ceased" | same |
| PNEUMA | "the manna stopped" | same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"the manna ceased"** | |
**Rationale:** Two-to-one agreement. The verb shavat carries the resonance of sabbath-rest and completion. "Ceased" preserves this gravitas. "Stopped" implies abruptness and loses the shavat overtone.

### D-14 | Verse 5:13 | "behold" vs. "look" vs. (omitted)
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "and look" | וְהִנֵּה |
| LOGOS | "and behold" | same |
| PNEUMA | (omitted — integrated into narrative) | same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"and behold"** | |
**Rationale:** Hinneh is a narrative presentative that signals surprise. It must be rendered, not absorbed. "Behold" preserves the heightened register appropriate to a theophanic encounter. "Look" is also valid but slightly more colloquial.

### D-15 | Verse 5:13 | "for our adversaries" vs. "on theirs"
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "or for our adversaries" | אִם-לְצָרֵינוּ |
| LOGOS | "or for our adversaries" | same |
| PNEUMA | "or on theirs" | same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"or for our adversaries"** | |
**Rationale:** Two-to-one agreement. Tsarenu ("our adversaries") is a concrete noun that must be rendered. "On theirs" loses the word entirely and weakens the binary whose refusal gives verse 14 its force.

### D-16 | Verse 5:14 | "No" vs. "Neither"
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "No" | לֹא |
| LOGOS | "No" | same |
| PNEUMA | "Neither" | same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"No"** | |
**Rationale:** Two-to-one agreement. Lo is a single syllable — the starkest negative in Hebrew. "Neither" is interpretive and softens the blow. The ki-clause that follows ("for I am the commander...") provides the clarification. The Hebrew does not say "neither" (lo zeh v'lo zeh); it says "No." The abruptness is theologically essential.

### D-17 | Verse 5:14 | "bowed down" vs. "in reverence"
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "bowed down" | וַיִּשְׁתָּחוּ |
| LOGOS | "bowed down" | same |
| PNEUMA | "in reverence" | same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"bowed down"** | |
**Rationale:** Two-to-one agreement. Vayyishtachu describes a physical action (prostration to the ground), not an inner disposition. The Hebrew narrates what the body does; the translator should not substitute motive for action.

### D-18 | Verse 5:14 | "What does my lord speak" vs. "What does my lord wish to say"
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "What does my lord speak to his servant?" | מָה אֲדֹנִי מְדַבֵּר אֶל-עַבְדּוֹ |
| LOGOS | "What does my lord speak to his servant?" | same |
| PNEUMA | "What does my lord wish to say to his servant?" | same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"What does my lord speak to his servant?"** | |
**Rationale:** Medabber is a participle — "speaking." PNEUMA's "wish to say" adds a volitional element not present in the Hebrew.

### D-19 | Verse 5:15 | Full prepositional phrase vs. abbreviated
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Remove your sandal from upon your foot" | שַׁל-נַעַלְךָ מֵעַל רַגְלֶךָ |
| LOGOS | "Remove your sandal from upon your foot" | same |
| PNEUMA | "Take off your sandal" | same (omits מֵעַל רַגְלֶךָ) |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Remove your sandal from upon your foot"** | |
**Rationale:** Two-to-one agreement. The phrase me'al raglekha is in the Hebrew and preserves the significant singular number (foot, not feet), distinguishing this from the plural in Exodus 3:5. Omitting it loses a textual datum.

### D-20 | Verse 5:15 | "is holy" vs. "holy it is"
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "holy it is" | קֹדֶשׁ הוּא |
| LOGOS | "is holy" | same |
| PNEUMA | "is holy" | same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"is holy"** | |
**Rationale:** Two-to-one agreement. The predicate-fronted Hebrew construction (qodesh hu) is a standard syntactic pattern. While EMET's preservation of the Hebrew word order is valuable in a literal translation, the consensus text normalizes to English word order without loss of meaning.

---

### LXX Divergences Noted by the Council

The Vaticanus manuscript (page 165, read directly) shows the following significant divergences from the Masoretic Text, all noted by the translators:

| Verse | MT (Leningrad) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Council Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 5:1 | "kings of the Canaanites" | "kings of Phoenicia" | LXX identifies Canaanites with Phoenicians — later geographical usage; MT followed |
| 5:1 | "their hearts melted" (vayyimmas) | "they were struck with terror" (kateplagēsan) | LXX weakens the visceral image; MT followed |
| 5:2 | "flint" (tsurim) | "stone" (petrinas) | LXX broadens; MT's specific "flint" followed |
| 5:9 | "I have rolled away" (galloti) | "I have taken away" (apheilon) | LXX destroys the galal/Gilgal wordplay; MT followed |
| 5:11 | "parched grain" (qalui) | "new grain" (nea) | Different description; MT followed |
| 5:14 | "commander" (sar) | "chief-commander" (archistrategós) | LXX elevates the military title; MT followed |
| 5:15 | singular "sandal/foot" | mixed number (singular sandal, plural feet) | LXX partially harmonizes to Exodus 3:5; MT singular followed |

### Qere/Ketiv Note — Verse 5:1

The Ketiv reads overenu ("until we crossed over" — first person plural); the Qere reads avoram ("until they crossed over" — third person). All three translators follow the Qere. The Ketiv preserves a striking perspective shift in which the narrator momentarily identifies with the crossing generation. The council follows the Qere as the standard reading but records the Ketiv as a remarkable textual witness.

### On the Singular Sandal — Verse 5:15

All three translators preserve the Masoretic singular na'alkha ("your sandal") and raglekha ("your foot"), noting the distinction from the plural in Exodus 3:5 (ne'alekha, raglekha — "your sandals ... your feet"). The LXX partially harmonizes to the plural. The council preserves the singular as it stands in the Leningrad Codex — it is the harder reading and therefore likely more original.

### On the Abrupt Ending — Verse 5:15

The chapter ends with vayyaas Yehoshua ken ("and Joshua did so") — without recording what the commander said next. The LXX includes additional material from Joshua 6:2–5 at this point, suggesting the chapter division is artificial. The MT's abrupt ending leaves the reader suspended in the encounter. All three translators note this. The council preserves the MT ending.

---

*Signed: SOPHIA (Σοφία — Wisdom)*
*In council with EMET (אֱמֶת — Truth), LOGOS (Λόγος — Word), and PNEUMA (Πνεῦμα — Spirit)*
*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), Pages 228–229, photographed by the West Semitic Research Project ((c) Bruce E. Zuckerman).*
